name: Wolf Pack
description: Canid social group demonstrating coordinated hunting, territory negotiation, and distributed care across multi-year relationship networks. Leadership is earned through social trust, not dominance alone.

scope.definition: Proportion of cooperative hunts resulting in a kill, for packs of 4-8 wolves hunting elk in open terrain.

evidence.source: Mech & Peterson (2003) Yellowstone reintroduction data; 10+ years post-reintroduction observation.

condition.environment: open grassland/forest edge, prey is elk or bison

scope.falsifiability: Success rate drops below 0.4 for solo wolves; pack fragmentation predicts hunt failure.
scope.measurement_limits: Rates differ significantly by prey type, season, and terrain. Dense forest reduces to ~0.45.

scope.definition: Proportion of pack members that coordinate movement within 200m during hunts, averaged across a hunting season.

evidence.source: GPS collar data from Yellowstone packs, 2005-2020.
cross_domain_examples: human team coordination in high-stakes operations

condition.environment: stable pack with established hierarchy and pair bond at center

scope.falsifiability: Cohesion drops below 0.6 after alpha pair death or pack size drops below 3.
scope.measurement_limits: Cohesion is seasonal; lowest during pup dispersal.

attributes.intergenerational_teaching: Pups learn hunt coordination through direct participation alongside experienced pack members. Skills are transmitted socially across generations without genetic encoding.
attributes.territory_negotiation: Howling broadcasts territory boundaries, reducing direct conflict. Scent marking creates persistent spatial memory readable by non-pack members.
attributes.communication_mode: Vocalization (howl, bark, whimper) + body posture + scent marking. Multi-modal, context-sensitive.
attributes.pattern: relational hierarchy, earned trust, multi-year social bonds, distributed care of young

links.note: Both demonstrate earned leadership, distributed decision-making, and multi-year relationship memory.
Octopus and wolf both solve novel problems via individual cognitive flexibility within a social frame.
Pack howls carry territorial memory across landscape; cultural knowledge encoded in behavior.
